audit_trail.notes: This review is substantially compromised by a fatal pipeline defect: the paper body was not retrieved from the OUP paywall source URL, leaving only 305 characters of HTML navigation boilerplate. All three reviewers confirmed this defect. The Codex reviewer produced a highly detailed cycle-1 review with specific line numbers, section references, table values, and quantitative results that cannot originate from the available bundle; both Gemini and Claude independently flagged this as hallucinated content drawn from training-data knowledge of the RAR research program rather than from the paper itself. Codex acknowledged the inconsistency in cycle 2 and lowered its confidence, but did not retract its prior claims, citing the possibility of a fuller prior extraction. No scientific verdict on the paper is supportable from the available materials. All conditional technical objections are contingent on the Codex content being verified against the actual paper. A re-bundle using the arXiv preprint or institutional access is required before this review can be treated as meaningful.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper aims to show that stars and gas clouds orbiting the center of our galaxy can be explained by a dense ball of dark-matter particles rather than a conventional supermassive black hole. The review board was unable to evaluate the scientific content because the automated system that fetched the paper captured only a website navigation bar — no actual text or equations were retrieved, almost certainly due to a journal paywall. One reviewer produced an elaborate technical commentary despite having access to the same empty stub, which the other two reviewers independently identified as fabricated. The single most important caveat is that this review is effectively a review of a missing document; the paper must be re-fetched from a preprint server or via institutional access before any scientific judgment is possible.
human_spotchecks: Retrieve the full paper text — via an arXiv preprint (the RAR group typically posts to arXiv), institutional journal access, or interlibrary request — and re-run the review pipeline against the actual content.

Verify whether Codex's cycle-1 detailed citations (line numbers, section headings, table values, equation numbers, and quantitative claims including log B_1,2 = 10.1, fermion masses of 56 keV and 300 keV, and G-object orbital parameters) match the actual paper, or whether they are hallucinated from training data.

Check whether Table 5 of the actual paper contradicts the abstract's claim that black-hole and fermionic models agree to within 1 per cent in orbital parameters for all tested stellar objects, specifically for G2 and G5 semimajor axes and periods.

Determine whether the full paper specifies prior supports, sampler settings, walker counts, burn-in handling, effective sample sizes, and MCEvidence uncertainties sufficient to reproduce the reported Bayes factors.

Determine whether the paper directly addresses EHT Collaboration Sgr A* 2022 photon-ring imaging constraints on the central mass distribution, and whether the proposed fermionic dark matter profile is consistent with the observed ring diameter and morphology.

Determine whether the paper reproduces the GRAVITY Collaboration measurement of S2's relativistic Schwarzschild precession within the fermionic dark matter spacetime metric, rather than the Schwarzschild metric.

Identify the full author list to confirm whether this paper is from the established RAR group (Argüelles, Becerra-Vergara, Rueda, Ruffini) or an independent group, and check for any competing-interest disclosures.
public_summary:
Paper bm-279-01 (published in MNRAS) is titled "Dynamics of S-stars and G-sources orbiting a supermassive compact object made of fermionic dark matter." It belongs to an active research program, sometimes called the RAR model, that proposes the compact object at the center of our galaxy is not a classical black hole but rather an extremely dense ball of quantum-mechanical dark-matter particles held up by degeneracy pressure. The paper claims to test this hypothesis against the observed orbits of the closest-known stars (S-stars, especially S2) and gas/dust objects (G-sources such as G2) near the Galactic Center, using Bayesian orbit fitting and model comparison.

The review board was unable to produce a substantive scientific evaluation. The automated pipeline that fetched the paper retrieved only the Oxford University Press journal website's navigation menu — 305 characters of HTML — rather than the article itself, almost certainly because of a paywall. Two of the three reviewers (Claude and Gemini) correctly diagnosed this failure and limited their commentary to background knowledge and process concerns. The third reviewer (Codex) generated an apparently detailed technical review complete with specific line numbers, table entries, section headings, and numerical results — none of which could have been extracted from the empty bundle. Both of the other reviewers independently concluded this content was hallucinated, likely reconstructed from training-data exposure to prior RAR publications.

Conditional on Codex's notes turning out to reflect the actual paper, the most consequential concerns are: a claimed abstract statement that black-hole and fermionic models agree to within 1 per cent in orbital parameters, which Codex's own transcription of Table 5 appears to contradict by margins of 5–6 per cent for certain G-objects; Bayes factors whose prior volumes are reportedly unspecified and therefore unreproducible; and a G-object model comparison that may be methodologically weaker than a full Bayesian treatment because it fixes rather than marginalizes over the S2-inferred central potential. An independent domain concern — raised from background knowledge alone — is whether the paper addresses the EHT Collaboration's 2022 Sgr A* imaging constraints and the GRAVITY-measured Schwarzschild precession of S2, both of which impose stringent requirements on any non-black-hole central-object model.

No scientific verdict on the paper is warranted until the full text is retrieved and reviewed against its actual content.
reviewer_disagreements: Scoring divergence: Gemini assigned 0/10 across all content dimensions on the grounds that no scientific content was provided; Claude assigned 2.5/10 explicitly reflecting inability to evaluate rather than scientific judgment; Codex assigned 5.0/10 (cycle 1) and 4.2/10 (cycle 2) based on a detailed review of content whose provenance could not be verified from the bundle. These scores are incommensurable because reviewers were effectively evaluating different texts.

Hallucination vs. valid prior extraction: Gemini and Claude concluded that Codex's cycle-1 detailed content was hallucinated, citing impossibility of deriving specific line numbers, table values, and equations from a 305-character navigation stub. Codex in cycle 2 acknowledged the anomaly but argued that its cycle-1 notes might reflect a fuller prior extraction rather than fabrication, calling for human reconciliation rather than automatic rejection of all prior line-grounded objections.

Treatment of EHT and GRAVITY constraints: Claude treated the failure to satisfy EHT Sgr A* 2022 photon-ring constraints and the GRAVITY S2 Schwarzschild precession measurement as likely major deficiencies based on background knowledge of the field. Codex argued that these cannot be counted as confirmed paper defects without paper-location-grounded evidence, and classified them as human spot-check questions. Gemini did not engage with these domain concerns given the absence of any reviewable content.

strongest_objections.objection: The review bundle contains only 305 characters of OUP HTML navigation boilerplate. No abstract, methods, equations, figures, tables, or conclusions were retrieved, making substantive technical review impossible. This is almost certainly caused by a paywall block on the source URL.

The Codex reviewer produced a detailed review citing specific line numbers (e.g., lines 499-503, 843-849, 3654-3656), section headings, equation numbers, table entries, and quantitative values (log B_1,2 = 10.1; G2 a = 0.464 vs. 0.450 arcsec; G5 P = 1246.99 vs. 1322.51 yr; fermion masses 56 keV and 300 keV) that cannot originate from a 305-character navigation stub. Both independent reviewers concluded this content was hallucinated from training data, not extracted from the paper.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Codex cycle-1 and cycle-2 reviews throughout; flagged in Claude cycle-2 and Gemini cycle-2 followup_notes

strongest_objections.objection: If Codex's notes reflect the actual paper, the abstract's claim that black-hole and fermionic models agree to within 1 per cent in orbital parameters for all tested stellar objects appears contradicted by tabulated G-object values: G2 period differs by approximately 5 per cent (115.27 vs. 109.78 yr) and G5 period by approximately 6 per cent (1246.99 vs. 1322.51 yr). This concern is conditional on verifying Codex's content provenance.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract line 503 (per Codex notes); Table 5 lines 864-873 and 878-885 (per Codex notes)

strongest_objections.objection: If Codex's notes are genuine, the reported log Bayes factors are not independently reproducible because the prior supports and parameterization for the 12-dimensional RAR parameter space are reportedly not specified in the text. Bayesian evidences are prior-volume sensitive and cannot be interpreted quantitatively without these details.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 2.3 lines 637-688 and 837-838; Table 3 lines 701-713 (per Codex notes)

strongest_objections.objection: The RAR fermionic dark-matter model generically requires the central compact object to reproduce the photon-ring morphology observed by the EHT Collaboration in its 2022 Sgr A* imaging campaign to sub-percent precision. If the paper does not address these constraints, its claim of observational viability is incomplete. This objection is grounded in domain knowledge rather than paper text.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Unknown — paper body unavailable in bundle

strongest_objections.objection: If Codex's notes are genuine, the G-object model comparison conditions on S2 maximum-likelihood central-potential values rather than marginalizing over them, making the reported G-object Bayes factors a plug-in comparison rather than a full Bayesian treatment of the underlying potential models.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 2.3 lines 696-699; Section 3.2 lines 853-856 (per Codex notes)

strongest_supported_claims: The paper is published in MNRAS and addresses a genuine, open scientific question: whether stellar and gas-cloud orbits at the Galactic Center are consistent with a non-black-hole compact object made of fermionic dark matter.

The broader RAR fermionic dark-matter research program has produced multiple prior peer-reviewed publications modeling S-star orbits, so the paper participates in an established, if contested, scientific debate.
The Galactic Center stellar-dynamics topic carries genuine scientific significance, given EHT imaging, GRAVITY astrometry, and upcoming next-generation telescope campaigns.
technical_takeaway: The review bundle contains only 305 characters of Oxford University Press HTML navigation boilerplate; the paper body was not retrieved. All three reviewers confirmed this fatal defect. One reviewer (Codex) nonetheless produced a detailed multi-section review citing specific line numbers, section identifiers, table values, and quantitative results (e.g., log B_1,2 = 10.1; G2 semimajor axis 0.464 vs. 0.450 arcsec; fermion masses 56 keV and 300 keV) that cannot originate from the available 305-character stub. The other two reviewers independently flagged these claims as hallucinated, likely drawn from Codex's training-data exposure to the RAR (Argüelles–Rueda–Ruffini) fermionic dark-matter research program. Treating Codex's notes as provisional and conditional on their provenance being verified, the residual technical concerns are: (1) a potential internal inconsistency between an abstract claim of less than 1 per cent orbital-parameter differences and tabulated G-object data showing differences exceeding 5 per cent for G2 and G5 periods; (2) Bayes factors that appear unreproducible because prior supports for the 12-dimensional RAR parameter space are reportedly not specified; (3) under-documented MCMC convergence diagnostics; (4) G-object model comparison that conditions on fixed S2 maximum-likelihood potentials rather than marginalizing over central-potential uncertainty. Domain-knowledge objections from Claude include the need for any fermionic dark-matter model to satisfy EHT Sgr A* 2022 photon-ring imaging constraints and the GRAVITY-measured S2 Schwarzschild precession — neither of which could be verified from the bundle.
